speaker: interviewer
text: What time is it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Else Dunner, maiden name Krieger, tape five. Mrs. Dunner, how long did you stay in California?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how-- [SIGHS] I-- I stayed there a few months. Six months I stayed in California, and I had got in trouble there right away, because they found out that that visa that we bought wasn't legal. And we had to get out. Otherwise we were afraid they will send us back to Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we needed someone-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] we found out that we needed someone to stay in California that really is a cousin. A cousin could guarantee for us to stay in-- in America. So we were-- we were looking for someone, and my cousin had there close friends in eastern Canada.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when she phoned and wrote to them, but they were not in town. They were not in town. So they phoned another one and asked where is he there. And he says, you don't need to ask for him. I will guarantee. A man that we never met, and my cousin didn't know, only he was a friend of a friend. And he says, don't worry, I'll guarantee. You don't need this one [INAUDIBLE] And sure enough, he guaranteed for me that he is a cousin. And then we could stay. And that's how we-- that is, another cousin and I-- we could-- could come to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come to Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came to Canada, and we-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] we had-- we had here a cousin. And he was very good to us too. He rented his apartment for us. You know, and we got a job, because my-- and then that cousin, he had a guy here that knew. So I got a job right away. And my cousin got a job as a-- like a nurse, like in-- in the hospital, like a helper. And we arrived. We got the apartment together, you know, and-- and we started to-- to live a normal life and to go out. And we wanted to go out. And we wanted to meet people and make friends here.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was a-- we found out that there was a-- a meeting from Council of Jewish Women for newcomers to come. So we went there because we want to meet people. And there we met-- we made friends with people who wanted to talk. And-- and they invited us right away, and we became very close friends with them. And they invited-- through them we met other people. And-- and one day, we were invited at another-- at other people's in the-- in the-- for a birthday party or something. And-- and this friend of mine said-- asked, don't you have someone for Else? Because you know, [LAUGHS] she took a liking to me She want me to-- So they said yes, my husband knows someone he met on the train. I'd have to ask him. My husband met someone, a nice young fellow, they say. [LAUGHS] And that was my husband. What was your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sam Dunner.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: You ask too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: You told me earlier that you got married in 1952?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's written down there? Then that's when I got married. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, with my husband I had-- yeah, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are the names of your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of-- of whose children?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Laura-- she was called after my-- my mother-- and Barry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure. I have-- Laura has three children, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Jaffa-- it's Jaffa and Aryeh and Shlomo. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: And Barry has two children?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Barry, he has two. Samuel is called after my father. My father didn't live long enough. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And Fanny, the girl, after her mother, Fanny.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Dunner, when you think over your life, how do you think your experiences of the war affected the way you live your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, for now I just take it the way it came. You know, I just have no-- no arguments. I have no nothing. I just take it now the way-- the way it come. I have nothing to-- to think about it. So I just take it the way it comes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think your experiences from the war affected the way you raised your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I never thought about it. I-- I really can't tell you. I don't think enough about that. I think-- I think it's more the way I was brought up at home that affected it more.

speaker: interviewer
text: You lost your mother. You lost your husband. Why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why I survived? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know whether I can call it a matter of luck. It was just meant to be. If I think I should call better-- it was meant for me to survive and talk through.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you had a message to leave to your grandchildren and the next generation, what would that message be?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a good question. I [? don't-- ?] I wouldn't know. I wouldn't know what to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you'd like to share with us today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right now I can't think of anything. I'm sure I can-- I will have lots of things to say, but right now I'm just blank.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to thank you very much for sharing your story with us today.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry I didn't-- didn't say enough and didn't-- but that's all I could do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is my grandfather, Moritz Krieger, and my grandmother, Channa Krieger, taken in 1920s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in-- in Bielitz, in my hometown. That is a picture of my mother, Laura, and me, Else or Elizabeth.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in 1916.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken-- the picture was taken in-- in Bielitz, in my hometown.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and this picture shows my father's family. On the right side is the youngest brother, Josef, and his wife, Malcha. And next to her is Mitzi, the sister-- only sister-- and my mother, Laura. Then on the right side again, starting on the right side is Mendel, is my auntie's husband. [BACKGROUND NOISE] Then my father's brother is Heinrich, and then Julius, the next one, and then Adolph, the next one, and then Zige, the next one.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The pictures was taken in 1923.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in my hometown when my uncle and auntie visited from the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a picture taken in 1923, when my uncle and auntie came from America to visit with us. That is a picture taken in [NON-ENGLISH]. And you can see there my-- my mother sitting and all our cousins and uncles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is your mother in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother is in the-- is sitting in the middle of the picture. And we the children, most of them are sitting on the floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am this picture too, on the left side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Second from  the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, second from the-- sitting next to my-- to my cousins.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Pardon? Is this a closeup of you of the same picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon? Yeah. Yeah, that is-- that is my cousin. I always felt he's my brother. We were the same age. Now, that is a picture of my first husband, Karl Zimmerspitz, and me. We were on a-- on a-- on a hiking tour.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in the near mountains from our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- it was in '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Barry is not allowed to talk, because his voice isn't legally identified.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is my number I got in Auschwitz, 764933. And I keep it, say, for good luck. Wherever I go, I keep it. OK. That is a picture of my son's wedding. From right to left, I am sitting. And next to me is Aryeh, my grandson, and next to him my husband, Sam. Then again from the right starting is my granddaughter, Jaffa. And-- and behind her is my son-in-law with  the baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your son-in-law's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shlomo. And then next to them is my daughter Laura. And at the very end is my son Barry.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't--

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: What is the year? No. Barry?

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, it was taken-- OK, thank you. I don't know what year. I tell him to be here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Next.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, this is a picture of my grandson Sam. He's Barry's son. And there is a little girl, Fanny.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken at home, here in Vancouver.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: In--

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1996, right. I would like to introduce to you my children. My daughter-in-law to my right, Susan, and my son to my left, Barry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thanks. And my sister, Laura Wasserman, is not here. Her and her family live in New York and-- and they couldn't make it. I'd just like to say a few words. My mother has been-- I have two children. And they're just babies, so they also couldn't make it. My mother has been involved in Holocaust education, telling her story for over 20 years here in Vancouver as part of a Vancouver Holocaust symposium. And since came out and it became quite known that she was on Schindler's List, she's been on local radio and television, and in many newspaper articles as well. And I'd like to thank the Shoah Foundation for doing this videotape so that her story and others like it will be broadcast and available to many people around the world. Thank you very much.
